João, I'll be direct. The Line 4 overrun is four weeks, and from our side it is clear: your team approved our interface drawings late. We're filing for an extension of time, and we expect Motiva to carry the cost.

I understand the concern, Carmen, and I've taken it seriously, I've been through the record in full. But I have to be honest with you: I dispute the claim. Before we talk about cost, let me put the dated sign-off log on the table.
Go ahead. But I'm telling you, our engineers were held up waiting on your approval.

With respect, the record doesn't support that. The drawings were approved on the fourth of March, countersigned by your own project engineer. The units left Shenzhen six weeks later, so the timeline attributes the overrun to the late delivery, not to us. On the log, the claim is unfounded.
Even so, we can't absorb a retest we don't think is ours. Someone has to pay for it.

I hear that. To some extent I understand the pressure you're under, but the burden of proof for the extension rests with the party requesting it, and I can't concede a point the record doesn't support. It is essential that any claim be substantiated from the log first.
So where does that leave us? I don't want to walk out of here with nothing.

Then here's a next step, not a verdict. Motiva reserves its rights under the penalty clause, but without prejudice, I recommend that both sides review the sign-off log together, and I'd propose we share the retest cost pending that review. It is critical that the commissioning date remain unchanged. Let's solve this together.
I understand the concern, and I've taken it seriously, but let me put the record on the table.
With respect, the record doesn't support that, the timeline attributes the overrun to the late delivery.
The burden of proof rests with the party requesting the extension, I can't concede what the record doesn't support.
It is essential that any claim be substantiated from the sign-off log first.
Without prejudice, I recommend that both sides review the log before anyone talks about a back-charge.

João, before you get on the call with the Spanish supplier this afternoon, let me tell you how I want you to run it, because the instinct under pressure is exactly the wrong one. They have filed an extension-of-time claim, and they are attributing the whole delay to us, to our documentation approval. The instinct, when a supplier says it is your fault, is to soften, to half-agree, to say well maybe we were a little late. Do not. The moment you concede a point you have not checked, the back-charge conversation is over and you have lost it. So here is the shape. First, acknowledge the concern, genuinely, once. Say you take it seriously and you have looked at it. That costs you nothing and it lowers the temperature. Then hold your ground on the facts. The burden of proof is on them, not on you, and you have the dated sign-off log, so use it. Do not raise your voice, do not get personal, just put the record on the table: the drawings were approved on the fourth, the units arrived six weeks after that, and the timeline attributes the overrun to the late units, not to us. Third, and this is the one people forget, stay diplomatic while you are being firm. It is essential that the tone stay professional, because you will work with these people again next year. You can dispute the claim and keep the relationship. And close with a next step, not a verdict. Propose that both sides review the log together before anyone talks about cost. That is holding your ground: firm on the facts, soft on the person, and always with a door still open.

Three things about defending a technical position when a supplier pushes a claim onto you, from someone who has sat through a lot of these disputes. First, separate the claim from the person. When Carmen says the delay is your fault, that is not an attack on you, it is a negotiating position, and it is her job to open high. The engineer who takes it personally starts defending his character instead of the facts, and the facts are the only thing that wins. So hear the claim as information about what she wants, not as an accusation, and your shoulders come down. Second, never concede what you have not verified, but always acknowledge what you have heard. These are not the same thing. Acknowledging costs nothing: I understand why you see it that way, let me look at the record. Conceding costs everything: yes, that was probably our fault. One keeps the burden of proof where it belongs, on the party making the claim; the other picks it up and carries it for them. And if a claim is genuinely unfounded, say so plainly but without heat: the record does not support that, and here is why. Third, the grammar of authority is quieter than you think. When you say, it is essential that the supplier submit the revised test plan before we proceed, you are not asking, you are stating a requirement, and the base form of the verb, submit, not submits, is what makes it sound like a norm rather than a favor. It is critical that the schedule remain unchanged. We recommend that the team be notified in writing. That is the language of a person who sets the terms, not one who reacts to them. Stay firm on the facts, warm on the relationship, and let the documented record do the arguing. You do not have to win the room. You only have to hold your position until the evidence does the work.
